sentences.text: No, not together, after that.
First my mother and my brother were arrested, then I a few hours later.
Yeah, I can describe that, because there were two SS [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] officers coming up to our apartment.
They drove a Red Cross car, a giant red cross on top of the car-- making sure that they wouldn't be bombed-- and drove me to the-- to the prison, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where we kept our clothes.
In fact, we could call to get a toothbrush or whatever [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sent.
See, I didn't tell you this, but the same lady who-- who ran the one-room-- the pension in which we had one room later lived with us in one room, in our apartment.
And so they notified her.
Then we got a sweater and a toothbrush after some time.
Anyway, we were obviously put into a Jewish section of the prison.
A cell that was probably made for two 2 3 people, now had 20, 25 people in it.
Very hard to breathe.

And-- and these were, most of them [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Polish and Hungarian Orthodox Jews, with beards, with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the works, prayed in their own way a great deal, you know, moving their bodies, traditionally sort of singing.
No.
No.
No.
No.
Just men.
No.
No.
There was just-- OK.
I should say I was taken into the same cell in which I found my brother.
So the two of us-- two of us were in this overcrowded cell.
And really, this was our first encounter of our lives with-- with people whose religion was Judaism [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and their prayers and whatnot.
And it was an interesting scene, because my brother and I, being very religious still at that time, spent a lot of time on our knees praying.
And everybody else around was swinging and singing.

And also, in order to get fresh air, just a little bit of fresh air, Max and I volunteered, I think daily, to-- to clean the halls with wet-- with wet-- with wet rags, so they let us out of the cell for cleaning purposes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] in ways which I don't remember now, we must have encountered somebody during the cleaning work who had seen my father.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We knew that he was also there.
And in fact, he sent a message to us whether we could get him some cigarettes.
He was a chain smoker.
And he wanted to swap a few slices of bread and get cigarettes from somebody, which of course we couldn't do.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Now, we also knew that my mother was there, obviously, because she was taken in, some other part of the prison, together with my brother.
So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that was it.
I mean, it was overcrowded.
It was obviously not very good food.
I don't remember.
I suspect it was, again, a bowl of soup a day, maybe a slice of bread.
But you see, you understand from my background, since we were free-- living freely until late July '44 without any economic restrictions, we were very sporty and-- and well-fed.
And I think undernourishment didn't hit us as much as people who had-- didn't have such a fortunate background.

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Really, the only nightmarish thing that I remember from the prison is when they were opening the cell door and three SS men came in and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] claimed that somebody had written a newspaper article hoping that the-- that the-- the street of one of the major avenues in Budapest called Andrassy was-- was going to be plastered with the heads of SS people.
It was probably purely imaginary, I imagine.
These guys were dreaming.
But anyway, so they came in and said, who wrote that?

Well, since obvious nobody wrote that, they picked on the-- on the most Jewish-looking guy in the crowd, who happened to be a-- a printer-- I'm sure he had nothing to do otherwise with journalism, he was nothing but a printer-- and nearly beat him to death in front of all of us, his blood flowing and all that.
I'll never forget his ears.
They were like-- like balls when it was finished.
This was done in the cell itself?
This was done in the cell, with 20, 25 people standing at attention.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes, easily.
I mean, it was not bigger than this room, probably smaller.
It was very crowded.
So you know, having not-- having had not a lot seen physical trauma before, this was a terrible day, which I'll never forget.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, the only other change from routine was, of course, there was a lot of fear about being called out, because people always felt that when they were called out, they were put in the courtyard and shot.
And-- and everybody was sort of ready for that.
Well, from the end of July until September, so that makes it August-- about six weeks.
No.
There was not a great deal of turnover.
No.
No.
But there was a lot of talk about being called out and people getting ready to it-- for it.
No.
I don't think anyone from our cell was shot while I was there.
But one day, of course, we were called, Max and I.
And we were taken in a car into the offices of the Gestapo, which was-- was in a villa in the most beautiful part of Buda.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's sort of interesting.
They-- there was a-- was a chap who dictated to his secretary a phony confession that we didn't want-- obviously, they called us Israel.
It was a law at that time.
All Jews had the middle name Israel, males, and Sarah for the women.
So they called us Andreas Israel, Chrambach, you know.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they took up a protocol, couple of pages of a protocol that the chap dictated to the secretary.
And the essence of it was that we had, first of all, not worn the yellow star and thereby violated the law, and secondly violated the law by-- by listening to enemy broadcasts.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after they dictated this, he said, well, you sign that.
Well, we signed it.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And what was actually sort of funny, you know, part of this interrogation, so-called-- I mean, of course it wasn't one-- had to do with the fact he wanted to convince us.
Well, he wanted to know what-- what sort of profession we were aiming for.
And not surprisingly, with his background, I told him that I'd always dreamt of becoming an army officer like my father.
So he said, well, that doesn't work.
You're Jews.
They wouldn't take you in the army.
But-- but maybe you can do something for us, to take us in the Waffen-SS, whether we would agree to that.
Well, obviously, we said we agreed to it very happily, would get us out of prison.
Probably not a wise choice had I known what these units were like, that they're just used to hunt up mines, clear minefields, with no-- no chance of survival.
So fortunately, it never materialized.

But somehow, this guy must have gotten the knack that he had these Jews in front of him with that phony offense, obviously, kids who just had been to high school, learned Latin and Greek, knew nothing about the world.

And-- and I think this must have to do with it, and, of course, also speaking fluent German and more than German because German, much more than an English, your class background reflects itself in the kind of language you speak.
So while these guys, of course, all spoke lower-class dialect, we spoke beautiful, high German.
I mean, that marked us instantly as being ruling class.

And I think, putting all of these together, we spoke proper German, we-- we-- we knew how to stand at attention, we had very military manners, you know, we talked with a clipped accent, the way they liked it, we were never tortured as many people were on these occasions, I think just for the fun of the interrogating officers.
So after we had signed this thing and they had kindly offered us the help in getting us into the Waffen-SS, we were sent home, put in a car, and sent back to the cell, never to be seen again.
But as it turns out, the fact that this two-page document existed really was very useful later, because that meant that the SS had a file called Andreas Chrambach.
And whenever you had a file, I think people were lots more careful not to let you disappear silently, as they could if the-- later in concentration camp, if there was no way to trace your existence.

So-- so I think I owe to that-- to that phony file the fact that when I finally arrived in Auschwitz, I was given, like everybody else, an identifying mark, which was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which was the-- the Jewish star.
But it was one yellow triangle superimposed on a red triangle.
And red triangles were-- were the mark for political prisoners.

So it made you what was called in the camp a political Jew, which-- which meant, as I just said, that-- that your chances of survival, at times, were probably better than if you were an unidentified-- unidentifiable transport Jude, transport Jew, as they were called, in the camp, who had no records whatsoever.
Something like six weeks.
Nothing, apart from cleaning the floors outside.
And I should say, sometimes, the floors had to be cleaned with toothbrushes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just to annoy us.
But we didn't care, because it took just longer time and we had more time to breathe fresh air.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was absolutely no-- no activity other than sitting, praying, watching.
You could-- could hardly move.
No.
No.
My brother and I were culturally and ethnically and so much out-- out of phase with everybody else.
We really didn't.
I mean, I remember one guy I was impressed with.
He-- he was sort of a free-thinker and had lived in Berlin and was a-- sort of a political nudist in the-- in the '20s, had enjoyed the free atmosphere of Berlin.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in fact, I think he was the only one who was not religious and talked a lot about need to stay firm when they finally put him against the wall.
But I don't remember his name.
Well, there was enough room just to-- like sardines, to stretch out next to the next guy.
On the floor, right.
Right.
Well, the prison situation ended by somebody opening the door and say, come out for transport.
And we were loaded onto-- onto a truck, back of a truck, with armed SS people standing at the exit, and were driven to the train station, put into a cattle car, and taken to Vienna.
Now, as I say, in this cattle car, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we also found my father and-- and my mother.
So actually, the four of us, for a few hours-- well, I don't know how long it took, maybe half a day-- could be together once more.
Yeah.
We were lined up, I think, before-- before actually getting onto the car, there were lineups where we could see them, of course.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
My father [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] instructed us, in fact, to take the best care we could of my mother, by [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] giving her as much hope and self-confidence as we could.
Well, I mean, that was even more crowded than the prison, in the sense that nobody could lie down.
I mean, there was just enough room for people to stand up like sardines.
It was really filled up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] yeah, that's it.
We didn't get food or drink or anything before we got to Vienna.
Yeah.
I think so.
I don't think anybody died in that-- in that transport.
It wasn't long enough.
And when we got to Vienna, we had to get out of the car and we lined up again.
There was one line for Mauthausen and one line for Auschwitz.
So that's when I saw my father once more, last time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he went to Mauthausen.
And we were then taken to a station in Vienna where the guards proceeded to feed us thin beer.
And I think it was out of their own pockets, because they felt-- felt sorry for people who hadn't seen water or anything probably for a day.

So-- and then the other thing that happened, we were taken to Auschwitz not in a cattle car, but in a normal, third-class [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wagon, railroad wagon, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with again I think it was two SS people, two guards, sitting at the door side of the compartment.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the thing I remember from that trip is that the guards urged us not to worry, that they said they had been not into Auschwitz, which was, obviously-- which was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] administered by other-- other troops, not-- not the guards from outside.
But they had gotten to the doors of Auschwitz.
And they told us not to worry about it, because they had seen factories there.
They had seen people working.
Probably nothing was going to happen for it-- to it.
And, of course, no one, no one would tell us or nobody knew and we didn't know it was an extermination camp.
And of course, in some ways, it wasn't.
I mean I will get to that.
Well, I mean, I knew-- people knew that this was the line for Mauthausen, whether or not they called it out.
How that news got around, I don't know.
But I remember, obviously, that he was lined up in the line other than ours.
My brother, my mother, and I, right.
We-- we traveled together in the same train compartment to Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's-- it's a considerable trip.
I don't remember exactly, but something like maybe 6, 8 hours, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Auschwitz being very close to Poland, very close to Katowice.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

So the next thing we knew, of course, on the other end of the trip-- I don't think-- I don't remember anything about that trip, other than-- than having these rather friendly guards who were really, obviously, tried to calm us down for whatever reason, maybe just to make their jobs easier.
But probably what it was-- I would suspect it was-- it was really genuine.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
We got to Auschwitz.
My mother was separated from us.
My brother and I were led into a compound that had to do with taking your clothes, having your clothes taken away, disinfection, delousing.
We were sprayed with delousing powder.
Your hair was shorn.
You were tattooed.
And of course, we hadn't shaved for quite some time, so I remember my brother and I looking at one another after we had put on these non-fitted clothes they'd thrown at us.
We looked exactly like the pictures in newspapers about the Russian partisans, you know.
And actually, we start laughing, because here were these spoiled high school kids, some-- converted by pure theatrical means into-- into 'the criminal class,' in quotes.
And I think my brother was 199813.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I should say it has no A or B, which has exactly the same meaning as the-- the red triangle I had talked about.
Anybody who was a transport Jude got an A or B in front of his number.
And when it came to selection later, I think that was important, because obviously everybody looked at your tattoo.

So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, the only other-- the only other thing I remember from that introduction to Auschwitz, other than the theatrical side and being shorn and so on, deloused, was that we looked out of a window and saw two or three SS men with cattle whips [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chasing a prisoner along the barbed wire.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think this incident and the one in the prison that I told you about are probably the only two incidences of brutality of that sort that I do remember.
But it made a profound impression on us to see this poor guy chased with the cattle whips.
All right.
The next thing was again a truck.
And we ended up-- now, the structure of Auschwitz, I can show you plans of it.
But it consists of-- of rectangular units, you know, maybe 20 of them, 30 of them, one next to the other, separated by barbed wire, and also separated by turrets, guard turrets with machine guns.
So-- so we were in one of these, driven into one of these, and, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK, given a place in-- in one of the blocks.
The blocks consisted of these wooden-- wooden beds, hundreds, one next to the other.
They were just wooden-- wooden cases filled with straw, where always two guys slept in-- in one box together.
And then I think we were woken up at 4:00, 4:00 in the morning or something like this, and recruited for labor, each labor battalion being led by a kapo.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK, if I said that I haven't seen any brutality in the camp except these two incidents, not true, just brutality by the SS.
In fact, we never saw any SS again much.
All the brutality in the camps was carried out by prisoners against prisoners, and of course, all the kapos, who did most of the brutality, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] most of the beatings.
A large majority, transport Jews.
A few-- one, I remember, was a political, Social Democrat German.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You see, most of them actually were fellow Jewish prisoners, who-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] who obviously got that for themselves, this position of supervisor, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for saving their own necks.
I mean it's, again, the story of the Aryanization of my father in a different-- different way.
And I should preface this by saying, in general, you know, I've read a lot-- a lot of claims about the solidarity of prisoners under those conditions.
And I must tell you I've never seen it.
I've heard about solidarity in the block occupied by Russian officers, Red Army officers.
But I'm pretty sure that was the only-- if-- if at all, that was the only group that showed any solidarity whatsoever.
The atmosphere in the camp was such that everybody was out for their own survival.
And if it was a question of-- of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] listening in to what your neighbors in the bed said, it would immediately be handed on so that-- for personal profit.
You couldn't trust anybody.
And nobody trusted anybody.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, the first-- the first brutality, I think, I saw was, in fact, by the-- in a-- in a-- in a work group that had to do with street construction.
And that's actually interesting, because it was actually led-- led by the social democrat from Frankfurt I told you about.
It was a guy was actually crippled and walked with a-- with a-- with a cane and used this cane extensively on the backs of the prisoners.
The reason was that he was given a quota for street construction every day.
His superiors, the SS whom we never saw, obviously told him he had to finish in a day two kilometers of street.
And since it was impossible to do by normal means, he simply beat his workers until he had his two kilometers.
That means, for another day, he saved his own neck.
Hadn't he fulfilled his quota, that would have been the last day he would've been a kapo.
There were a lot of advantages, of course, of being a kapo.
So-- so that was the general-- that-- that was one of 'em.
And of course, then another thing I remember were, you know, you-- you kept your shoes and your clothes under the hay where you slept, because otherwise everybody would steal it.
And one day, in my block, the-- a Portuguese Jew stole shoes of somebody else and was caught.

And he was severely beaten by the blockalteste who was a French parliamentarian with whom, later, I became actually quite close, by name of Paul Self, who simply felt that one could not condone one prisoner stealing from another.
But this guy was terribly beaten.
So-- so you saw that sort of thing, but not from the SS, because the SS was not to be seen.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I-- I would say, OK, the only time I remember seeing an SS man was when I was caught by him talking over the fence to the next rectangular camp, which was occupied by women.
And in fact, there was a Hungarian woman, and I think all I was doing, in total innocence, is to-- is to practice my Hungarian with her.
Well, it was obviously not allowed to talk across this.
So the guy appeared.
It was a steep slope before you got to the barbed wire.
I was at the bottom of the slope.
The guy was on top.
And so he called me to come up.
And of course, I did this.
In my best Prussian self, I stood absolutely immobile at attention while he ripped off some of the things that I had sewn on badly.
There were exact regulations where your number had to be sewn and what not.
Yeah.
